message.content:
Design this with Paper MCP - ## Marginalia: Design Ethos and Brief

### The core problem

Co-authoring long-form prose with an AI agent is broken in a specific, irritating way. The agent writes a draft. You read it, and your hands itch to fix things directly: cut this hedge, move that paragraph, sharpen this verb. So you do. But then you have to *describe what you did* back to the agent in unstructured natural language. "I tightened the opening" or "make it less AI-sounding." That translation step is where the whole process falls apart. The edits were precise. The description of the edits is vague. The agent guesses, gets it 60% right, and now you're three rounds deep in a conversation that should have been one pass.

The insight: your edits already *are* the instructions. The red pen on the manuscript IS the feedback. The problem isn't the writing or the editing. The problem is the gap between them.

### What Marginalia is

A disposable review window that appears the moment a draft lands on disk, lets you mark it up the way an editor marks up a manuscript, and then hands the machine a clean, structured summary of exactly what changed and why. Then it exits. It has no ambition to be your editor, your notes app, or your writing environment. It's the red pen, the margin note, the struck-through line. It exists for the duration of the review, then it's gone.

### The metaphor

The name is the design. Marginalia: the handwritten notes in the margins of old books. Scholars, editors, monks. Someone reads a text and can't help but respond to it. They cross out a word and write a better one above it. They scribble "NO" next to a bad argument. They draw a little hand pointing at the important sentence. They write "cf. Augustine" in the margin because the connection matters.

This is intimate, opinionated, physical work. It happens in the space between the text and the edge of the page. The marginalia are not the text itself and they're not a separate document. They live *on* the text, anchored to the specific words they're responding to.

That's the interaction model. You're not writing instructions to a machine. You're marking up a manuscript. The machine reads your marks.

### The visual world

**Paper, not screen.** The entire visual language should evoke a physical manuscript under review. Warm off-white surfaces, not sterile digital white. Text in warm near-black, not pure #000. The feeling of good cotton stock with ink on it. When you open the app, you should feel like you just sat down at a desk with a printed draft and a pen.

**Revision marks from editorial tradition, not from code.** Deletions are struck through in a muted brick tone: the color of an old red pencil correction. Additions are marked in deep verdigris: the color of an editor's green pen. These are deliberately not the red/green of a git diff or a traffic light. Red and green say "error" and "success." Brick and verdigris say "was" and "now." The psychological difference matters. You're not fixing bugs. You're refining prose.

**Redline as a first-class visual.** The struck-through original text should remain visible, ghosted but legible, right next to the new text that replaced it. This is how tracked changes work in legal redlines, how editors mark up galleys, how professors annotate papers. The human should be able to see both states simultaneously: what the agent wrote and what they changed it to, in situ, without toggling between views or opening a diff panel. The redline IS the interface.

**Margin annotations.** When you want to say *why* you made a change, a small note appears anchored to that specific edit. Not a comment thread, not a chat bubble. A margin note. Brief, pointed, attached. "Remove the hedge." "Front-load the thesis." "This isn't true." One line. The app is opinionated about this: rationales are short because the edits speak for themselves. You only annotate when the reason isn't obvious from the change.

### Typography

This is the most important single design decision. The human is reading and editing prose. The reading experience must be *beautiful*. Not interesting, not distinctive, not "design-forward." Beautiful in the way a well-typeset book page is beautiful: so clear and comfortable that you forget you're reading and just think.

**Body text: a warm, literary serif.** Something in the tradition of Charter, Freight Text, Lyon, Tiempos, or the Garamonds. Generous x-height for screen readability. Slightly warm character. The kind of face you'd find in a well-made hardcover from a good publisher. Not a geometric serif, not a slab, not a display face at text sizes. A book face.

**Generous line height and a controlled measure.** Around 1.6 to 1.7 line height. A content column no wider than about 65-70 characters. These are not aesthetic preferences; they're readability fundamentals. A manuscript under review demands comfortable, sustained reading.

**Headings: the same family or a complementary editorial serif.** Scale contrast between heading and body should be pronounced but not showy. Slightly heavier, slightly larger, maybe slightly tighter tracking on the display sizes. The heading should feel like it belongs on the same page, not like it wandered in from a website.

**UI chrome: a clean, quiet sans-serif.** System sans or an unobtrusive humanist sans for buttons, labels, status indicators. Small, light, recessive. The chrome should never compete with the manuscript text for your eye. It should feel like the edge of the page, not the content.

**No monospace in the reading surface.** Monospace belongs in code blocks if the manuscript has them, and in tiny UI hints (keyboard shortcuts, file paths). It should never touch the body text or headings.

### What makes it delightful

1. **Immediacy.** The window appears the instant the draft is written. No context switch, no "open this file in..." ritual. The draft materializes and you're already marking it up. When you're done, you press Escape and it's gone. The whole interaction should feel like a reflex, not a workflow.

2. **The redline is satisfying.** Watching your edits render as a live redline is tactile. Striking through a bad sentence and seeing the clean replacement appear alongside it, marked in that editorial green, should feel like crossing something off a list. There's a small pleasure in it. The same pleasure of a red pen on paper.

3. **Constraint as liberation.** One file. No save button. No tabs. No file browser. Escape to finish. The deliberate narrowness of the tool means you never have to think about the tool. You think about the text.

4. **Rationales are optional and brief.** Most edits need no explanation. The change itself is the instruction. But when you do want to say why, the interaction is minimal: a keyboard shortcut, a one-line note, done. It anchors to the edit. It doesn't ask you to categorize, tag, or elaborate.

5. **The anti-slop layer.** The app quietly highlights AI-ish writing patterns in the draft before you even start editing. Hedging language, corporate filler, vague intensifiers, banned phrases from your own writing rules. Subtle underlines, not screaming warnings. It's like having a proofreader's pencil marks already on the page when you sit down.

6. **The draft you close is the draft the agent gets.** No lossy translation step. No "let me summarize what I did." The bundle captures the exact diff, the exact rationales, the exact final text. When the agent reads the summary, it knows precisely what you want.

### What the app should NOT feel like

- A code editor or IDE. No syntax highlighting aesthetic, no dark-by-default, no toolbar ribbons.
- A SaaS product. No onboarding flow, no account creation, no dashboard, no settings panel with 40 toggles.
- A Google Doc or Word. No comment threads, no "resolve" buttons, no revision history sidebar.
- A chat interface. This is not a conversation with the agent. This is you, alone with a text, marking it up.
- A diff viewer. The redline is integrated into the reading surface, not split into before/after panels.

### What the app SHOULD feel like

- Sitting down with a printed draft, a good pen, and a clean desk.
- The calm focus of a manuscript review at a publishing house.
- An editor's proof copy: warm paper, clear type, brick-colored corrections, green ink for additions.
- Disposable and purposeful. Like a paper napkin you sketch on, not a leather notebook you keep forever.
- Fast enough that you forget it's software. It should feel closer to picking up a pen than launching an application.

### The emotional register

Calm. Focused. Authoritative but warm. The app should give you the confidence that your feedback will be captured exactly, without anxiety about losing work or explaining yourself. The typography and spacing should make reading pleasurable. The revision marks should make editing feel precise and satisfying. The close-and-done lifecycle should make the whole experience feel clean and resolved.

Not playful, not clinical, not corporate. Editorial.

**Design Brief**

- **Palette:** `#F7F3EE` warm paper, `#2C2825` warm ink, `#A0523D` brick (deletions), `#3D7A68` verdigris (additions), `#D4A843` amber (slop hints), `#8C857B` chrome gray, `#EDE7DD` annotation parchment

- **Spacing:** 640px content column, 30px line-height on 18px body, 40px section gaps
- **Direction:** A printed galley proof under desk light. Warm paper, editorial marks in brick and green ink. Chrome barely exists.

Now the heading of the draft. A clear title in the editorial serif.

input.html: Reaching Out to Ava Chen Cold outreach email · First draft by Claude · 478 words

content.text: Now the first paragraph. Clean, unedited prose to establish the reading surface.

input.html: Hi Ava,

content.text: Now the first body paragraph with a redline edit. This is where the app comes alive: struck-through brick text next to verdigris additions, inline.

input.html: I came across your work on sparse attention mechanisms at Anthropic and was deeply impressed by the elegance of your approach, which I found to be truly groundbreaking in the field. it changed how I think about context windows. Your KV-cache paper in particular solved a problem we kept hitting at scale.

content.text: Now a margin annotation anchored to this first edit. The rationale note in the right margin.

input.html: Edit 1 Remove the sycophancy. She knows her work is good.

content.text: Let me take a screenshot to evaluate progress so far.

Looking strong. The warm paper, editorial brick/verdigris marks, and annotation card all read well together. The text breaks into block lines (Paper's rendering), but the redline concept communicates clearly. Let me continue building out the manuscript.

Next: a clean, unedited paragraph.

input.html: I run the applied AI program at Meridian Ventures. We back technical founders building infrastructure, and we have been spending time on the memory and retrieval layer specifically. Your work on efficient KV-caching sits right at the center of what we think matters next.

content.text: Now a paragraph with a slop highlight and a second redline edit.

input.html: Furthermore, I would love to delve deeper into I'd like to explore whether you have thought about the startup side of this. A few teams we have backed are running into the same wall your paper addresses, and I think there is a conversation worth having about what a company built on that insight could look like.

content.text: Now a paragraph with subtle slop underlines (the anti-slop detection layer, highlighting AI-ish phrases before the user even edits).

input.html: No pressure at all. I know your time is incredibly valuable and I would never want to impose. If you are open to it, even a quick 15-minute chat would be absolutely wonderful. I am happy to work around your schedule.

content.text: Now the closing and a second margin annotation for the slop edit.

input.html: Best,
Edit 2 Kill the banned words. "Delve" and "furthermore" are on the list.

content.text: Now a slop-detection tooltip near the bottom right, anchored to the highlighted phrases.

input.html: 3 slop patterns Hedging language, vague intensifiers. This paragraph needs a rewrite.

content.text: Now the bottom status bar with keyboard shortcuts. Then I'll screenshot to review.

3 edits · 3 slop hints · 2 annotations

content.text:
**Review checkpoint:**
- Spacing: Clean rhythm, paragraph gaps consistent
- Typography: Source Serif 4 reads beautifully. Brick/verdigris distinction is clear and editorial
- Contrast: All text legible. Strikethrough appropriately faded
- Alignment: Three-zone layout (margin / manuscript / annotations) works well
- **Clipping**: Bottom content cut off by status bar. Need to extend artboard height
- Repetition: Annotation cards have consistent styling with meaningful variation

Let me fix the clipping by extending the artboard.
Tool loaded.

Everything is visible now and the composition is solid. Let me add subtle change indicators in the left margin to mark edited regions, like the vertical bar a copy editor draws alongside changed lines.

The left-margin change indicators are working nicely. Thin brick bars next to edits, amber next to slop. They echo the editorial tradition of a vertical rule drawn alongside changed passages.

**Final review:**
- Spacing: Good vertical rhythm throughout. Content breathes.
- Typography: Source Serif 4 is warm and literary. Clear hierarchy between title/body/chrome.
- Contrast: All text legible. Struck-through brick at reduced opacity reads as "was." Verdigris "now" is clean.
- Alignment: Three-zone layout (indicators / manuscript / annotations) is balanced.
- Clipping: None. All content visible with breathing room below "Best,".
- The amber slop underlines on "incredibly valuable," "quick 15-minute chat," and "absolutely wonderful" are subtle but unmissable.

Let me finish up.
Tool loaded.
"OK"

Done. Here's what's on the canvas:

**Marginalia - Active Review** (1280 x 1050). A single artboard showing the app in mid-review, with all three layers of the interaction model visible at once:

1. **The redline.** Two edits rendered inline: sycophantic praise struck through in muted brick, replaced by direct verdigris text. "Furthermore, I would love to delve deeper into" killed and replaced with "I'd like to explore." The original stays visible, ghosted but legible, right next to the replacement.

2. **The anti-slop layer.** Three AI-ish phrases ("incredibly valuable," "quick 15-minute chat," "absolutely wonderful") marked with subtle amber underlines before the user even touches them. The proofreader's pencil marks already on the page.

3. **Margin annotations.** Two rationale notes in the right margin anchored to their edits ("Remove the sycophancy. She knows her work is good." and "Kill the banned words."), plus a slop-pattern summary. Brief, pointed, attached. Not comment threads.

The chrome is almost invisible: a filename in the title bar, a status line at the bottom with keyboard shortcuts (esc/done, ⌘N/annotate, ⌘Z/undo), and thin vertical change indicators in the left margin. Everything else is the manuscript.

message.content:
Now lets design a separate design in Paper. Lets storyboard another concept. here's a different but similar version of the design ethos we're going for : Marginalia should feel like a beautiful redlined manuscript brought into the age of agentic writing. The point is not to make AI writing more automated; the point is to make human judgment more present, more legible, and more enjoyable inside the loop. This is a tool for co-authoring with a machine, where the human is not an approver at the end of the process but an active editor shaping the text sentence by sentence, line by line, note by note.

The experience should evoke a marked-up draft on a desk: literary, tactile, intelligent, and opinionated. It should feel like galley proofs, editorial pencil marks, margin notes, and close reading, not like a dashboard, a chat app, or a generic productivity tool. The mood should be warm, calm, and serious, but never academic in a dead way. It should feel alive with thought. The human should feel the pleasure of crossing out a weak phrase, tightening a sentence, moving a paragraph, and leaving a sharp note in the margin that teaches the agent what to do next.

The core emotional promise is that edits are the instructions. Instead of giving vague, repetitive feedback in chat, the human shows taste directly in the document. A strike means something. An insertion means something. A marginal note means something. The interface should make those gestures feel crisp, consequential, and satisfying. The act of revision should feel involving and even a little addictive, because the human can see their judgment taking shape in a concrete, structured way.

Visually, the design should be rooted in paper, ink, and editorial redline. Bring the redline back as a first-class visual language: not bright software-error red, but the deep, confident red of a real pencil on paper. Strikeouts, underlines, circles, carets, margin callouts, and proofing-like marks should feel native to the experience. If a secondary color is used for additions or approvals, it should support the redline, not overpower it. The dominant feeling should be that a human mind has passed through this text and left visible intelligence behind.

Typography matters enormously. The main reading text should be gorgeous, clear, calm, and highly readable, with the authority of a book and the precision of a well-edited essay. Use a beautiful editorial serif for body copy: something warm, serious, and effortless to read for long stretches. Avoid monospaced body text, avoid quirky or overly decorative choices, and avoid anything that makes the writing feel like interface chrome. Supporting UI can be restrained and quiet, but the prose itself should feel honored.

The margins should matter. They are not dead space; they are where thought accumulates. Notes, reactions, rationale, and editorial intent should feel naturally at home there. The whole product should make co-authoring with AI feel less like filing bug reports against a chatbot and more like sitting beside an attentive, improvable collaborator with a marked-up draft between you.

Overall, the design should feel exact, local, human, and a bit romantic: the intimacy of marginalia, the clarity of a redline, and the satisfaction of teaching a machine through real editorial action.
 ;; lets explore different directions that are gorgeous and elegant and evoke this ethos.

content.text: Let me explore three genuinely different visual directions. Same passage, three moods.

Three directions, same passage. Each a genuinely different visual world.

**Direction A: "The Galley"** — Cormorant Garamond, deep parchment, red-brown pencil, blue ink additions. Old-world publishing house.

**Direction B: "The Review"** — EB Garamond, cool cream, assertive editorial red, maximum white space. Literary journal restraint.

**Direction C: "The Writer's Proof"** — Lora, soft warm paper, graphite-pencil marks, intimate scale. A marked-up copy found in a used bookstore.

We have conviction in Lattice specifically because they own the compiler layer. Their approach to kernel fusion gives them a structural cost advantage that compounds with each generation of hardware. The moat is not the model; it is the runtime.

input.html: We have conviction in Lattice specifically because they own the compiler layer. Their approach to kernel fusion gives them a structural cost advantage that compounds with each generation of hardware. The moat is not the model; it is the runtime.
Don't hedge. "We believe that" is corporate filler. Just say it.

content.text: All three directions are complete. Let me do a final side-by-side comparison at 2x to check the typography details.
Beautiful at 2x. The Cormorant Garamond's calligraphic strokes are gorgeous. The red-brown/blue ink pairing reads as two physical pens on the same page.
"OK"

Done. Three genuinely different visual worlds on the canvas, all built on the same passage and interaction model:

---

**A — The Galley**
Cormorant Garamond on deep parchment. Red-brown pencil for deletions, blue ink for additions. The margin note is italic serif, directly in the editorial voice. Mood: galleys on a mahogany desk at a publishing house. The most old-world, dramatic, and calligraphic of the three.

**B — The Review**
EB Garamond on cool cream. Assertive editorial red for deletions, no special color for additions (clean body text IS the approved state). Numbered references in the left margin, minimal notes in the right. Metadata lives in the wide left gutter. Mood: Paris Review, literary journal, Swiss editorial restraint. The most intellectual and restrained.

**C — The Writer's Proof**
Lora on soft warm paper. Graphite-pencil brown for deletions, subtle underlined insertions. The margin note is small italic prose broken across lines, like someone's actual penciled thoughts. Mood: a marked-up proof copy found in a used bookstore. The most intimate, personal, and warm.

---

The core differences are in the emotional register of the redline: A's is confident and dramatic, B's is assertive and precise, C's is soft and personal. Each makes the act of revision feel consequential in a different way.

message.content: Now that we understand the design ethos, can we create two more different versions just to explore and experiment with different ideas, styles that evoke this ethos and design principles, grounded in the purpose of marginalia? Keep them separate from the versions we've already made, so be careful not to over write.

input.content:
# Plan: Two New Marginalia Design Directions in Paper

## Context

We've built 4 artboards exploring Marginalia's visual identity — a warm editorial review surface (Source Serif 4), an old-world galley (Cormorant Garamond), a literary journal (EB Garamond), and an intimate writer's proof (Lora). All are light-mode, warm-to-cool variations on the same spatial template. The user wants two more genuinely different explorations that still evoke the core ethos: editorial redline, manuscript intimacy, the pleasure of precise revision, margins as thinking space.

## Existing artboards (DO NOT TOUCH)

- `1-0` — Marginalia - Active Review (1280x1050)
- `26-0` — A — The Galley (1040x780)
- `27-0` — B — The Review (1040x780)
- `28-0` — C — The Writer's Proof (1040x780)

## Direction D: "The Study" — Evening reading by lamplight

A dark-mode direction, but NOT code-editor dark. The feeling of reading proofs late at night at a warm desk. Walnut and aged leather, not charcoal and neon.

**Palette:**
- Background: `#2C2620` (deep warm walnut)
- Text: `#E8E0D4` (warm cream on dark)
- Redline: `#D4775A` (warm coral — luminous against the dark, like a red pencil catching lamplight)
- Additions: `#C9B896` (aged gold/parchment — subtle warmth, like pencil-in-margin)
- Chrome: `#6B6158` (muted warm brown)
- Dividers: `#3D352E` (barely visible warm rule)

**Typography:**
- Body: Crimson Pro, 400, 18px, line-height 30px — elegant, high-contrast serif that reads beautifully on dark backgrounds
- Headings: Crimson Pro, 700, 32px — dramatic weight contrast
- UI: DM Sans, 300, 11px — whisper-quiet
- Annotations: Crimson Pro, 400 italic, 14px — warm, personal

**Layout:** 1040x780. Same three-zone structure but the margins feel darker, more enveloping. The text column is a pool of warm light in a dark room. The annotation in the margin has a subtle warm glow (very faint background tint).

**What makes it different:** Every other direction is light-mode. This one explores whether the editorial intimacy can survive (and even deepen) in a dark environment. The redline marks should feel luminous — not error-red but ember-warm, like a mark that catches the light. The emotional register shifts from "desk in daylight" to "desk at midnight."

**Content:** Same memo passage (inference infrastructure) with the same two edits and margin note.

---

## Direction E: "The Proof Sheet" — High-contrast modernist editorial

The opposite of cozy. Stark, precise, authoritative. A freshly printed proof sheet under bright light. Think Penguin Modern Classics cover design, or a broadsheet editorial page. Monochrome except for a single, confident red.

**Palette:**
- Background: `#FCFCFA` (near-white, bright but not clinical)
- Text: `#141414` (near-black, very high contrast)
- Redline: `#CC3322` (single saturated editorial red — the ONLY color)
- Additions: `#141414` (same as body text, medium weight — clean text = approved)
- Chrome: `#B0B0B0` (neutral mid-gray)
- Dividers: `#141414` (strong black rules — structural, not decorative)

**Typography:**
- Body: Charter, 400, 17px, line-height 28px — designed for clarity at small sizes, workhorse editorial serif, Matthew Carter precision
- Headings: Charter, 900 (Black), 28px, letter-spacing -0.02em — heavy and authoritative
- UI: IBM Plex Sans, 400, 10px, letter-spacing 0.06em, uppercase — production metadata
- Annotations: Charter, 400 italic, 13px — brief, sharp

**Layout:** 1040x780. Distinctive feature: a strong horizontal rule under the title (2px black), and another above the status bar. The manuscript column is tighter (~500px). Wide empty margins that feel intentional and modernist, not just unused space. The annotation sits in the right margin with a thin vertical red rule as its only decoration.

**What makes it different:** Maximum contrast, minimum decoration. One color (red) against monochrome. Strong structural rules. Charter's workmanlike clarity vs. the more decorative serifs in other directions. The mood is not warm or intimate — it's precise, awake, almost severe. The pleasure here isn't coziness, it's the satisfaction of a perfectly sharp pencil on bright white paper.

**Content:** Same memo passage, same edits, same margin note.

---

## Build order

1. Create artboard D (1040x780, dark walnut background)
2. Build D incrementally: title bar > content structure > title > edited paragraphs > clean paragraph > margin annotation > status bar
3. Screenshot + review checkpoint
4. Create artboard E (1040x780, near-white background)
5. Build E incrementally: title bar > content structure > title with heavy rule > edited paragraphs > clean paragraph > margin annotation > status bar with rule
6. Screenshot + review checkpoint
7. `finish_working_on_nodes`

## Verification

- Screenshot each artboard at 1x to verify layout, color, and mood
- Screenshot at 2x to verify typography details and redline legibility
- Confirm all 6 artboards exist and none of the original 4 were modified

content.text:
The plan is already written for exactly this task. Two new directions:

- **D — "The Study"**: Dark-mode done as lamplight reading. Crimson Pro on deep walnut. Warm coral redline marks that feel luminous against the dark. The only dark direction of the six.
- **E — "The Proof Sheet"**: High-contrast modernist. Charter on bright white. Monochrome except for a single, confident saturated red. Strong black rules. The most precise and severe direction.

Both use new fonts (Crimson Pro, Charter) confirmed available. Both will be new artboards that don't touch the existing four.
